title: Mechanism of monosodium fumarate in inhibiting Bacillus cereus spore growth and its implications for food safety control.
author_string: Li, Yangfu, Li, Xiang, Wang, Juan, Chen, Ling, Li, Yuecong et al.
abstract: Bacillus cereus, a ubiquitous foodborne pathogen, represents a critical threat to food safety owing to its ability to produce highly resilient spores that can withstand conventional food processing techniques. This underscores the urgent need for effective strategies to control B. cereus spores in the food industry. Although fumaric acid and its salts are well-known for their broad antibacterial properties against vegetative bacteria, their impact on spores remains poorly understood. In this study, we present the first evidence of the inhibitory effects of monosodium fumarate (MF) on the growth of B. cereus spores. Our findings demonstrated that MF effectively inhibits spore proliferation without killing or structurally damaging the spores, primarily by preventing spore germination. Mechanistic investigations revealed that MF interferes germination by suppressing the release of monovalent cations, which in turn disrupts the signal transduction necessary for germination. These findings were further supported by membrane potential measurements and dipicolinic acid release analyses. Furthermore, MF significantly inhibited B. cereus spore growth in dairy beverages and pork products, demonstrating its potential for practical application in two major food matrices susceptible to B. cereus contamination. This study not only provides the mechanistic insights into MF's action on spore germination but also lays a solid foundation for its use in the food industry.
